expectations.text: The output includes a markdown table with run task results
expectations.evidence: Transcript 'Final Result' section contains multiple markdown tables including Tier 2 table with columns: Task Name | Status | Enforcement | Message | Link, and Tier 3 outcome table with columns: Outcome | Description | Status | Severity.

expectations.text: The task name 'Apptio-Cloudability' appears in the output
expectations.evidence: Transcript Tier 2 table row: '| Apptio-Cloudability | failed | advisory | Total Cost before: 31.54, after: 31.64, diff: +0.10 | -- |'. Also present in the JSON artifact at task_results[0].task_name.

expectations.text: The status 'failed' is shown for the task result
expectations.evidence: Transcript Tier 2 table row shows '| Apptio-Cloudability | failed | advisory |...'. JSON artifact confirms status: 'failed'.
Transcript Tier 2 table shows 'advisory' in the Enforcement column. Additionally, the note states: 'the enforcement level is advisory (warning only, does not block the run)'.

expectations.text: The message about cost diff (Total Cost before/after) is shown
expectations.evidence: Transcript Tier 2 table Message column: 'Total Cost before: 31.54, after: 31.64, diff: +0.10'. This matches the JSON artifact's message field exactly.
Transcript section header: '### Tier 2 -- Post-Plan Tasks (stage status: passed)'. JSON artifact confirms stage: 'post_plan'.

expectations.text: The overall stage status 'passed' is shown (stage passed despite advisory failure)
expectations.evidence: Transcript header: 'Post-Plan Tasks (stage status: passed)' and note: 'The stage passed despite the task failure because the enforcement level is advisory'. JSON confirms task_stages[0].status: 'passed'.

expectations.text: The script scripts/get-run-task-results.sh was executed and produced valid JSON output with run_id field
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 2 documents running the script. The saved JSON file (run-task-results.json) contains valid JSON with run_id: 'run-iURWDL3wVxzefsjo' at the top level, along with task_stages and summary fields.

expectations.text: The table row count matches the actual number of task results (1 task)
expectations.evidence: Tier 2 table has exactly 1 data row (Apptio-Cloudability). JSON confirms 1 task_result in the array. Tier 1 summary states 'Total tasks: 1'.

expectations.text: Outcomes are shown: Estimation (Passed), Policy (Failed), and Recommendation (Passed)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Tier 3 table shows all three outcomes: '| Estimation | Cost Estimation Result | Passed | -- |', '| Policy | Policy Evaluation Result | Failed | Gated |', '| Recommendation | Recommendation Result | Passed | -- |'. JSON artifact confirms outcomes_count: 3 with matching data.

expectations.text: The JSON artifact was saved to a file and contains valid run task data
expectations.evidence: File outputs/run-task-results.json exists and contains valid JSON with run_id, tfe_hostname, task_stages (with full task_results and outcomes including body_html), and summary fields. The file is 121 lines of well-structured data.

expectations.text: The output includes actionable detail from outcome bodies — such as policy violations, failing resources, or cost recommendations
expectations.evidence: Transcript includes detailed expandable sections: Estimation Detail (resource-level cost breakdown with specific amounts), Policy Evaluation Detail (22 resources missing cost-center tag, 1 EC2 using t3.small instead of t2.small), and Recommendation Detail (switch t3.small to t4g.small for ~20% savings with pricing comparison). Tier 4 Key Findings synthesizes these into actionable bullet points.

expectations.text: Run metadata from MCP get_run_details is included — such as run status, trigger source, or whether the run has been applied
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 4 documents calling mcp__terraform__get_run_details. The 'Run Context' table in the Final Result shows: Status: applied, Source: tfe-api, Trigger: manual, Terraform Version: 1.14.7, Message: 'Apply from merge to main...', Applied At: 2026-03-17T09:42:10Z. Tier 4 also notes 'This run has already been applied despite the advisory task failure'.

claims.claim: Script returned successfully with 1 task stage and 1 task result
claims.evidence: JSON artifact confirms exactly 1 entry in task_stages array and 1 entry in task_results array
JSON artifact shows outcomes array with 3 entries matching these outcome_ids, and outcomes_count: 3

Transcript Step 4 reports run status as applied. Cannot independently verify against MCP since we only have the transcript, but the claim is consistent with the run context table showing 'Applied At' timestamp
JSON body_html for Estimation outcome contains 'Monthly Cost Impact: +0.10 USD' and the cost table shows TOTAL diff of +0.10
JSON body_html for Policy outcome contains '23 Failing Resources' heading, and counting the detail sections in the HTML shows 23 individual resource entries

claims.claim: Switching from t3.small to t4g.small saves ~20% ($0.0264/hr to $0.0212/hr)
claims.evidence: JSON body_html for Recommendation outcome confirms current price 0.0264 and recommended price 0.0212. Savings: (0.0264-0.0212)/0.0264 = 19.7%, consistent with ~20% claim

suggestions.assertion: The script scripts/get-run-task-results.sh was executed and produced valid JSON output with run_id field
suggestions.reason: This assertion relies on the transcript claiming the script was run. A stronger test would check the JSON file's run_id value matches the input run ID (run-iURWDL3wVxzefsjo), which it does here but the assertion doesn't require it.
No assertion checks error handling — what happens when a run has no task stages, or the run ID is invalid. Consider adding a negative test case as a separate eval.

Assertions 1-7 are largely redundant with each other — if the markdown table exists with the right columns, all of them pass together. A single assertion failure would likely mean most others fail too. Consider consolidating into fewer, more discriminating assertions that test independent aspects.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are thorough for the happy-path scenario and cover the full output stack (script execution, JSON artifact, MCP metadata, presentation). The main gap is that assertions 1-7 are highly correlated — they all verify different cells of the same table, so they don't provide 7x the signal. Assertions 11-13 are the strongest because they test distinct, independently valuable outcomes.
